explanation: The natural language statement says that once all self-tests are completed successfully, it must be possible to enter Standby Mode. This means that after 'selfTestPass' becomes true, at some point it is possible to enter 'StandbyMode'. In LTL, this can be captured by stating that whenever selfTestPass becomes true, then eventually StandbyMode should be true (i.e., 'StandbyMode' should hold at some point after self-test is passed). The most common way to express this is: 'G(selfTestPass -> F StandbyMode)', which enforces that globally, whenever selfTestPass holds, StandbyMode is eventually enabled. This matches the intent that Standby Mode becomes accessible upon successful self-test, but imposes no precondition for StandbyMode except selfTestPass, and does not require StandbyMode to be entered immediately nor prohibits later entries. Previous LTLs that did not include the implication or the eventuality operator would not enforce that StandbyMode follows selfTestPass, nor would they avoid unnecessarily limiting StandbyMode access before self-test (which isn't covered by the requirement).

The natural language statement specifies that after all self-test steps have been completed successfully (represented by selfTestPass), it must become possible to proceed to the Standby Mode (represented by StandbyMode). In LTL, 'once p, it shall be possible to q' is commonly represented as 'after p holds at any time, eventually q must become true at some point afterwards.' This is captured as: G(selfTestPass -> F StandbyMode), meaning 'Globally, whenever selfTestPass becomes true, StandbyMode should eventually become true afterwards.' This LTL formula correctly captures the intent that, once all self-test steps are passed, moving to Standby Mode is possible and must eventually occur if desired. No numerical comparison is used, only atomic propositions.

The natural language states that after all self-test steps have been completed successfully, it shall be possible to proceed to Standby Mode. In LTL, this is captured by specifying that it is always the case that if 'selfTestPass' occurs, then eventually 'StandbyMode' will become true – that is, passing all self-test steps guarantees that Standby Mode can be reached. The formula G(selfTestPass -> F StandbyMode) enforces that whenever the self-test passes, StandbyMode can eventually be entered. Previous incorrect LTL translations might require StandbyMode to directly follow selfTestPass immediately, which is not stated in the natural language (the timing/order is not strict 'next', but eventual possibility).

The input states: 'Once all self-test steps have been completed successfully, it shall be possible to proceed to the Standby Mode.' The atomic propositions relevant are 'selfTestPass' (all self-test steps passed successfully) and 'StandbyMode' (system is in Standby Mode, i.e., Ventilation Off Mode).

The phrase "Once all self-test steps... completed successfully, it shall be possible to proceed to the Standby Mode" is causally linking successful self-test as a prerequisite for entering Standby Mode, meaning StandbyMode cannot occur before selfTestPass, but is possible any time after selfTestPass. A standard LTL translation for such a "no StandbyMode until selfTestPass" property is: 'not StandbyMode until selfTestPass'. This states: globally, it is not possible for StandbyMode to be true before selfTestPass first becomes true; after selfTestPass has occurred, StandbyMode may become true or false at any time (no further restrictions).

A tempting but incorrect formalization would be 'selfTestPass -> F StandbyMode', i.e., 'after selfTestPass, eventually StandbyMode will occur': the input does not require that StandbyMode _must_ occur after selfTestPass, only that it _may_ occur, i.e., that StandbyMode strictly requires selfTestPass to be true first.

Therefore, the correct property is:

!(StandbyMode) U selfTestPass

The requirement is that, after ALL self-test steps have been completed successfully, proceeding to Standby Mode becomes possible. From the propositions, 'selfTestPass' indicates all self-test steps passed successfully and 'StandbyMode' indicates the system is in Standby Mode. The natural language implies that entering Standby Mode is only allowed after the self-test is successfully passed: that is, 'StandbyMode' can only occur after 'selfTestPass' has occurred at least once. In LTL, the property 'G(StandbyMode -> F[0:1] selfTestPass)' would mean that whenever the system enters Standby Mode, the self-test must have passed in the current or previous step. However, to exactly require that Standby Mode cannot occur before selfTestPass, the standard LTL idiom is '!StandbyMode U selfTestPass', but this only ensures StandbyMode doesn't happen before selfTestPass, not that it is possible once it passes. To express that after all self-tests have passed, then it is possible (permitted) to enter Standby Mode, but before that, Standby Mode MUST NOT occur, it is most accurate to say: '(!StandbyMode) U selfTestPass'.

Moreover, if we want to express that eventually self-test will pass and then Standby Mode may happen any time after, it's 'F(selfTestPass) & G( !StandbyMode -> !selfTestPass )', but since the question doesn't require eventuality, the 'U' idiom suffices.

Thus, the chosen LTL is '!StandbyMode U selfTestPass': Standby Mode cannot occur until after selfTestPass has become true.

The requirement states that once all self-test steps have been completed successfully (as indicated by the atomic proposition 'selfTestPass'), it must be possible to proceed to Standby Mode (represented by the atomic proposition 'StandbyMode'). In LTL, this is captured by the property 'G(selfTestPass -> F StandbyMode)'. This means that globally (at all times), whenever the 'selfTestPass' condition holds (i.e., the self-tests are completed successfully), then eventually, Standby Mode can be reached at some point afterward. This directly realizes the requirement that completion of all self-tests enables the option to proceed to Standby Mode. No previous LTL property is provided, so there is no need to compare with others for possible shortcomings.

The natural language statement, 'Once all self-test steps have been completed successfully, it shall be possible to proceed to the Standby Mode,' describes a requirement that Standby Mode may only be entered after the self-test passes. In LTL, this is best captured by expressing that it is never allowed to enter Standby Mode before the self-test has passed. This can be written as: 'it is always the case that, until selfTestPass occurs, StandbyMode does not occur.' In formal LTL: '!(StandbyMode) U selfTestPass'. After selfTestPass, StandbyMode is allowed (but not necessarily enforced immediately). Previously, one might be tempted to use something like G(selfTestPass -> F StandbyMode), but this would require StandbyMode to eventually follow selfTestPass, which isn't implied by the requirement—it only stipulates that StandbyMode is not allowed before selfTestPass. Thus, '!(StandbyMode) U selfTestPass' is correct and captures the intent.

The input requirement states that, after all self-test steps have been successfully completed, it must be possible to proceed to Standby Mode. In LTL, this is best captured by guaranteeing that once 'selfTestPass' holds, then eventually 'StandbyMode' becomes possible (i.e., StandbyMode holds at some point after selfTestPass). This does not require StandbyMode to happen immediately after the self-test, only that it becomes possible at some point afterwards. The formula 'G(selfTestPass -> F StandbyMode)' expresses that globally (at all times), if 'selfTestPass' occurs, then in the future 'StandbyMode' will occur. This matches the given natural language and does not impose any additional constraints or mistakes, such as requiring StandbyMode before selfTestPass, or adding any numerical comparisons (which are prohibited).

The natural language requirement specifies that it shall only be possible to enter Standby Mode after all self-test steps have been completed successfully. The atomic proposition 'selfTestPass' represents the completion of all self-test steps, and 'StandbyMode' represents being in Standby Mode. To capture the constraint that Standby Mode can only be entered after selfTestPass becomes true, we formulate the property such that StandbyMode cannot occur before selfTestPass has occurred. This is done by saying 'not StandbyMode' must hold until 'selfTestPass' occurs; after 'selfTestPass', StandbyMode may follow. This is written as: '(!StandbyMode) U selfTestPass'. This ensures it is never possible to have StandbyMode true before selfTestPass is true at least once. Other LTL formulas might allow StandbyMode before selfTestPass or might require StandbyMode immediately after selfTestPass, but the requirement is only that it shall be possible (not mandatory) after selfTestPass, not before.

The input statement says: 'Once all self-test steps have been completed successfully, it shall be possible to proceed to the Standby Mode.' Based on the available atomic propositions, 'selfTestPass' indicates that all self-test steps passed successfully, and 'StandbyMode' indicates the system is in Standby Mode. The specification is about a possibility: after passing the self-test, entering Standby Mode becomes possible (permissible). In LTL, we can express a temporal dependency such that: it is not the case that StandbyMode can occur before selfTestPass occurs; StandbyMode can only occur after selfTestPass. This is best captured with the LTL formula: '!selfTestPass U StandbyMode -> F(selfTestPass)' (i.e., StandbyMode cannot occur until selfTestPass has happened). However, more succinctly, we can express: Whenever StandbyMode holds, it was necessarily preceded by selfTestPass; i.e., StandbyMode implies that at some point before (or at the same time), selfTestPass occurred. In standard LTL, this is expressed with the release (R) or with 'G(StandbyMode -> F[0:1] selfTestPass)'. Since the event is about allowing, and LTL cannot directly express possibility, we encode the safety property: StandbyMode cannot occur before selfTestPass. So the best encoding (without action-level atomic props) is: '!StandbyMode U selfTestPass', or equivalently: G(StandbyMode -> P(selfTestPass)), but we lack P (previously), so we use the until formula.

Therefore: '!selfTestPass U StandbyMode -> F(selfTestPass)' ensures that StandbyMode can only occur after selfTestPass has happened, accurately capturing the intent of the sentence.
